tweets.text:
that’s like 6 consecutive Black Friday’s, which is a 36σ event 

a one-tailed 36σ event has probability ≈ 4×10⁻²⁸⁴, or about 1 in 2.4×10²⁸³

To calibrate how absurd that number is: there are ~10⁸⁰ atoms in the observable universe, and ~10⁶⁰ Planck times have elapsed since the Big Bang. If every atom drew one sample every Planck time for the entire age of the universe, you’d have ~10¹⁴¹ draws and the odds of seeing even one 36σ outcome would still be around 1 in 10¹⁴².

Which is the real takeaway when this comes up in markets: observing a “36 sigma move” is evidence of a cosmically rare event. Would Gauss lie?
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: The S&P 500 is 1.8% below its all time high currently

tweets.text: @RubenRicOk @aggresivevalue The CLSA number you’re quoting is for 2027

thread_tweets.text: @RubenRicOk @aggresivevalue The CLSA number you’re quoting is for 2027
thread_tweets.full_text: @RubenRicOk @aggresivevalue The CLSA number you’re quoting is for 2027

thread_tweets.text:
UBS 2027 Industry Revenue = $1760B, dram under supplied until mid 28 but SKH/samsung fabs ramp 2H27, micron Idaho online mid-27 so moderate growth from 27-28, put it at 8% base case = $1900B

Attribution rate, CLSA has memory as a % of hyperscaler capex at 48% for 2027, implied attribution of 30% of memory industry revenue that lands in hyperscaler capex. Mix shift continues as supplier mix shifts to HBM/server DDR5/eSSD, extrapolating attribution at 37% 2028e. ~704B hyperscaler attributed memory spend.

Let’s say ~$1200B hyperscaler capex for 2028. That’s about 9% growth from 2027.

704/1200=0.587 

You can play around with memory growth / mix premium / capex growth, I think unless you have a really out of consensus estimate on any of those you’re landing between 55-60%.
thread_tweets.full_text:
UBS 2027 Industry Revenue = $1760B, dram under supplied until mid 28 but SKH/samsung fabs ramp 2H27, micron Idaho online mid-27 so moderate growth from 27-28, put it at 8% base case = $1900B

Attribution rate, CLSA has memory as a % of hyperscaler capex at 48% for 2027, implied attribution of 30% of memory industry revenue that lands in hyperscaler capex. Mix shift continues as supplier mix shifts to HBM/server DDR5/eSSD, extrapolating attribution at 37% 2028e. ~704B hyperscaler attributed memory spend.

Let’s say ~$1200B hyperscaler capex for 2028. That’s about 9% growth from 2027.

704/1200=0.587 

You can play around with memory growth / mix premium / capex growth, I think unless you have a really out of consensus estimate on any of those you’re landing between 55-60%.

thread_tweets.text: But yeah again to my point - if the market wants to put a 10x earnings multiple on 2028 at 52% net margin, then the memory oligopoly probably should double. But it’s also easy for the market to say “well maybe <supply catches up to demand/architectures use legacy memory more effectively/efficiency breakthrough/insert any number of things that can happen by 2028>” and change their mind. The variance in perceived outcomes is where the volatility comes from.
thread_tweets.full_text: But yeah again to my point - if the market wants to put a 10x earnings multiple on 2028 at 52% net margin, then the memory oligopoly probably should double. The variance in perceived outcomes is where the volatility comes from.

tweets.text:
Kim Sunwoo of Meritz Securities: "This Is Not the Time to Sell Samsung Electronics and $SKHY"

They claim markets are excessively misunderstanding the situation with semis. And that DRAM shortage will intensify in the second half of this year.  

- H2 2026: "suppliers can fulfill only 75%–80% of DRAM demand". 
- 2027: "fulfillment is expected to fall into the 60% range."

In the article, they attached market forecasts that show SK Hynix with a 2027 3.5x P/E and Samsung a 3.9x P/E. 

Think the $INTC CEO said it best around timeframes with his quote "no relief in memory supply or pricing until at least 2028". 

Especially after $MU 16 LTAs with favorable take or pay volume contracts... memory demand seems structural.

tweets.text:
kimi k3 feels more significant than the deepseek moment. hard to unsee it:

> beats fable, got 5.6 on multiple key benchmarks. 1st time a chinese model *doesn’t* trail

> it does so for a fraction of the size, cost and without the swanky nvidia gpus

> repeated breakthroughs in data used to train models +  training techniques have made china a legitimate centre for ai research

all of this is open source too. at what point do we start acknowledging they might actually have some advantage here?

gap has closed to 3 months tops now.
tweets.full_text:
kimi k3 feels more significant than the deepseek moment. hard to unsee it:

> beats fable, got 5.6 on multiple key benchmarks. 1st time a chinese model *doesn’t* trail

> it does so for a fraction of the size, cost and without the swanky nvidia gpus

all of this is open source too. at what point do we start acknowledging they might actually have some advantage here?

gap has closed to 3 months tops now.

tweets.text:
hate to say it but it looks like china's closed the gap to US frontier models

this is the 1st chinese model that looks, feels and works like a frontier model. it's also the 1st time a chinese model has beat fable 5, gpt 5.6 at multiple difficulties.

Kimi k3 is within striking distance in coding, reasoning, visual intelligence and agentic work, open source but also cheaper than sonnet 5

another thing: the taste. it actually seems to have an engaging personality that intuitively produces aesthetic work.

i need to play around with this more but initial reactions is im surprised they made this.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Introducing Kimi K3: Open Frontier Intelligence

🔹 2.8 Trillion Parameters, 1 Million Context, Native Multimodal
🔹 Kimi Delta Attention enables up to 6.3x faster decoding in million-token contexts
🔹 Attention Residuals deliver ~25% higher training efficiency at <2% additional https://t.co/eFHEbdxn3P
